title: CA: Civil Construction Induction
subtitle: Entry-Level Safety and Compliance — Canada
description: Essential knowledge for civil construction workers in Canada. Covers provincial OHS legislation, WHMIS 2015, Red Seal program, TAC standards, and key safety requirements for BC, Ontario, and Alberta.

lessons.title: How OHS Works in Canada
lessons.content:
**Canada Has No Single National Construction Safety Law**
Unlike Australia or New Zealand, Canada's occupational health and safety legislation is primarily provincial. Each province has its own OHS Act.

**Federal vs Provincial Jurisdiction:**
- Federal Canada Labour Code Part II covers federally regulated workers (airports, rail, banks, telecoms) — less than 10% of workers
- Provincial/territorial legislation covers the vast majority of civil construction workers

**Key Provincial Acts:**
- British Columbia: Workers Compensation Act + OHS Regulation (WorkSafeBC)
- Ontario: Occupational Health and Safety Act (OHSA) + O. Reg. 213/91 (Construction)
- Alberta: Occupational Health and Safety Act 2017 + OHS Code 2025

**Universal Principle — Internal Responsibility System (IRS):**
All Canadian jurisdictions operate under the IRS principle — everyone in the workplace shares responsibility for safety. Workers have the right to:
1. Know about workplace hazards
2. Participate in health and safety
3. Refuse unsafe work

lessons.title: Worker Rights — Refuse Unsafe Work
lessons.content:
**The Right to Refuse Unsafe Work — All Provinces**

Every Canadian worker has the legal right to refuse work they believe poses an unusual danger to themselves or others.

**Refusal Process (Ontario Example — OHSA s.43):**
1. Worker refuses — reports to supervisor
2. Supervisor investigates with worker present
3. If not resolved — Ministry of Labour inspector called
4. Inspector investigates and makes written decision
5. Worker may continue to refuse until investigation complete

**Key Rule — No Reprisals:**
It is illegal for an employer to discipline, penalise or threaten a worker for refusing unsafe work.

**Joint Health and Safety Committees (JHSC):**
Required on most construction projects with 20+ workers. Must have:
- Equal employer and worker representation
- At least one certified member each side
- Monthly meetings on projects over 3 months
- Written recommendations to management

questions.q: In Canada, construction safety is primarily regulated by:
options: The Canada Labour Code for all workers

questions.q: The Internal Responsibility System means:
options: Only the employer is responsible for safety
Everyone in the workplace shares safety responsibility
Workers are responsible only for their own area

questions.q: Canadian workers have the legal right to:
A Joint Health and Safety Committee is generally required when a project has:

lessons.title: WHMIS 2015 — Workplace Hazardous Materials
lessons.content:
**WHMIS 2015 is Mandatory Training for ALL Canadian Workers**

WHMIS (Workplace Hazardous Materials Information System) 2015 is Canada's national hazard communication standard, aligned with the international Globally Harmonized System (GHS).

**Two Types of Hazardous Products:**
1. Physical hazards (flammable, explosive, oxidising, compressed gas)
2. Health hazards (toxic, corrosive, carcinogenic, sensitising)

**Three Key Elements of WHMIS:**
1. Labels — on every hazardous product container
2. Safety Data Sheets (SDS) — detailed safety information
3. Worker Education and Training

**The 9 WHMIS 2015 Hazard Pictograms:**
- Flame (flammable)
- Flame over circle (oxidising)
- Exploding bomb (explosive)
- Corrosion (corrosive to metal/skin)
- Skull and crossbones (acute toxic)
- Exclamation mark (irritant/harmful)
- Gas cylinder (pressurised gas)
- Health hazard (carcinogen/sensitiser)
- Environment (aquatic hazard — voluntary in Canada)

**Key Rule:** Never use a product if you cannot find or read its SDS.

lessons.title: Hazardous Materials in Civil Construction
lessons.content:
**Common Hazardous Materials on Canadian Civil Sites:**

**Silica Dust**
- Released when cutting concrete, rock, masonry
- Causes silicosis — irreversible lung scarring
- Canadian OEL (Occupational Exposure Limit): 0.025 mg/m3 (respirable fraction)
- Controls: Wet methods, local exhaust ventilation, N95/P100 respirator

**Asbestos**
- Found in infrastructure built before 1990
- Must not be disturbed without testing
- Requires designated substance plan in Ontario
- Licensed asbestos abatement contractor required for removal

**Diesel Exhaust**
- Major hazard in tunnels and confined spaces
- Contains Group 1 carcinogens (IARC)
- Controls: Tier 4 engines, ventilation, monitoring

**Lead**
- Found in old painted structures, some soils
- Provincial designated substance requirements for abatement

questions.q: WHMIS 2015 requires training for:
options: All Canadian workers who may be exposed to hazardous products

questions.q: A Safety Data Sheet (SDS) must be:
options: Kept in the main office only
Accessible to workers at the work site

questions.q: The skull and crossbones WHMIS pictogram means:
Silica dust is a risk when:
options: Cutting or grinding concrete, rock, or masonry

lessons.title: Heavy Equipment Operator — Red Seal
lessons.content:
**The Red Seal Program (Interprovincial Standards)**

Canada's Red Seal Program allows certified tradespeople to work across provinces without retesting. It is administered by Employment and Social Development Canada (ESDC).

**Heavy Equipment Operator (HEO) — Red Seal**
The HEO trade is a Red Seal designated trade in most provinces. However, it operates differently from NZ or AU:

- Red Seal HEO is voluntary — not legally required to operate equipment
- Employers may require it for hire or advancement
- Demonstrates verified competency to national standard

**HEO Equipment Categories:**
- Excavator
- Dozer
- Grader
- Scraper
- Loader
- Backhoe loader
- Compaction equipment

**Provincial Apprenticeship Pathways:**
- BC: SkilledTradesBC — 3 year apprenticeship
- Ontario: Ontario College of Trades — Operating Engineers
- Alberta: Alberta Apprenticeship and Industry Training

**Operating Engineers (Unionised):**
In many Canadian provinces, heavy equipment operators on large projects must be members of the International Union of Operating Engineers (IUOE) — Local 115 (BC), Local 793 (Ontario), Local 955 (Alberta).

lessons.title: Fall Protection and Working at Heights
lessons.content:
**Fall Protection — Canadian Requirements**

Falls are the leading cause of death in Canadian construction. Specific requirements vary by province:

**Ontario (O. Reg. 213/91):**
- Fall protection required at 3 metres (10 feet) on construction sites
- Working at Heights training mandatory (MOL-approved provider)
- Training must be renewed every 3 years

**British Columbia (OHS Regulation Part 11):**
- Fall protection required at 3 metres
- Written fall protection plan required for unguarded areas

**Alberta (OHS Code Part 9):**
- Fall protection required at 3 metres for general construction
- 7.5 metres for some specific work types

**Fall Protection Hierarchy:**
1. Eliminate the fall hazard (design out)
2. Passive systems (guardrails, covers)
3. Fall restraint system
4. Fall arrest system (harness + lanyard — last resort)

**Key Difference from NZ/AU:** Canada's fall protection trigger is 3 metres (NZ and AU: 2 metres).

questions.q: The Red Seal HEO certification is:
options: Legally required to operate an excavator
Voluntary but demonstrates national competency standard
Only valid in the province it was issued

questions.q: In Ontario, Working at Heights training must be renewed every:
Fall protection in Ontario is required at:
The preferred (highest priority) fall protection method is:
options: Eliminate the fall hazard by design

lessons.title: TAC and CSA Standards
lessons.content:
**Transportation Association of Canada (TAC)**
TAC is the national body for road transportation knowledge in Canada, equivalent to Austroads in Australia/NZ.

**Key TAC Publications:**
- Geometric Design Guide for Canadian Roads
- Manual of Uniform Traffic Control Devices for Canada (MUTCDC)
- Pavement Asset Design and Management Guide
- Guide for Bridge Temporary Works

**Canadian Standards Association (CSA) — Key Standards:**
- CSA Z1000-14 — Occupational Health and Safety Management Systems
- CSA Z1001-18 — Occupational Health and Safety Training
- CSA Z96 — High-Visibility Safety Apparel
- CSA Z259 series — Fall Protection equipment
- CSA G40.20/G40.21 — Structural steel

**Provincial Road Authorities:**
- BC: Ministry of Transportation and Infrastructure (MoTI)
- Ontario: Ministry of Transportation (MTO)
- Alberta: Alberta Transportation

**Call Before You Dig:**
Each province has its own utility locate service:
- BC: BC 1 Call (1-800-474-6886)
- Ontario: Ontario One Call (1-800-400-2255)
- Alberta: Alberta One Call (1-800-242-3447)

lessons.title: Traffic Control — Canadian Requirements
lessons.content:
**Traffic Control on Canadian Roads**

**Manual of Uniform Traffic Control Devices for Canada (MUTCDC)**
Published by TAC — sets national standard for signs, signals, pavement markings, and temporary traffic control.

**Traffic Control Person (TCP) Certification:**
Anyone directing traffic on Canadian roadwork sites must hold a Traffic Control Person certificate (province-specific).

**Provincial TCP Requirements:**
- BC: TCP certification through approved provider, valid 3 years
- Ontario: TCP training (no formal certification but training required)
- Alberta: Traffic Control Person certificate required

**Standard Minimum PPE for TCP:**
- CSA Z96 Class 2 or 3 high-visibility vest
- Hard hat
- Safety footwear (CSA Grade 1)
- Stop/Slow paddle (MUTCDC standard)

**Key Rule:** Traffic Control Plans must be prepared for all work affecting traffic flow.

questions.q: The Canadian equivalent of Austroads is:
CSA Z96 covers:
Before excavating in British Columbia you call:
The MUTCDC is published by:

lessons.title: Course Summary
lessons.content:
**Congratulations — You have completed CA: Civil Construction Induction!**

Key knowledge covered:

- Canadian OHS is primarily provincial — each province has its own Act
- Internal Responsibility System — everyone shares safety responsibility
- Three worker rights: Know, Participate, Refuse unsafe work
- WHMIS 2015 is mandatory training for all workers
- Red Seal HEO is voluntary but nationally recognised
- Fall protection trigger: 3 metres (vs 2 metres in NZ/AU)
- TAC = Canadian equivalent of Austroads
- Call Before You Dig varies by province

**Key Differences vs NZ:**
- No single national construction safety law (provincial system)
- WHMIS is the equivalent of NZ's HSNO regulations
- Fall protection at 3m not 2m
- Red Seal = voluntary HEO qualification (no equivalent in NZ)

options: Is protected by law — no reprisals allowed
